epigraph.text:
Where two or three gather in my name, there am I 
with them.
epigraph.quote: The whole is more than the sum of its parts.

content.text: {The whole is more than the sum of its parts.}{Aristotle}

When two lines cross, they meet at exactly one point—a point that 
belongs to both lines. This simple geometric fact is the foundation of 
solving systems by graphing.

content.title: System of Linear Equations
content.content:
A **system of linear equations** consists 
of two or more linear equations considered together. A **solution** 
of the system is an ordered pair that satisfies *all* equations 
in the system.

content.title: Verifying a solution
content.problem:
Determine whether $(3, 2)$ is a solution of the system:
\[
\begin{cases}
 2x + y = 8 \\
 x - y = 1
\end{cases}
\]
content.solution:
Substitute $x = 3$ and $y = 2$ into each equation:
$$
 2(3) + 2 &= 6 + 2 = 8 \quad \checkmark \\
 3 - 2 &= 1 \quad \checkmark
$$
Since $(3, 2)$ satisfies both equations, it is a solution of the system.

content.text:
The graphical method relies on a key insight: the solution to a system 
is the point where the graphs of all equations intersect.

content.title: Solving Systems by Graphing
content.content:
1. Graph each equation on the same coordinate plane.

2. Identify the point of intersection.

3. Check the solution in both original equations.

content.title: Solving by graphing
content.problem:
Solve the system by graphing:
\[
\begin{cases}
 y = 2x - 1 \\
 y = -x + 5
\end{cases}
\]

content.text:
When equations are in standard form, convert to slope-intercept form 
or use intercepts to graph.

content.title: System in standard form
content.problem:
Solve by graphing:
\[
\begin{cases}
 x + y = 4 \\
 2x - y = 2
\end{cases}
\]

content.title: Accuracy of Graphing
content.content:
Graphing is excellent for visualizing systems but has limitations:

- The intersection point may not have integer coordinates.

- Reading fractional coordinates from a graph is imprecise.

- Errors in graphing lead to incorrect solutions.
For precise answers, algebraic methods (substitution, elimination) 
are preferred.

content.title: Simultaneous Equations
content.content:
Systems of equations appear in ancient mathematics. Chinese mathematicians 
of the Han dynasty (around 200 BC) used a method similar to modern 
elimination in the text *Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art*. 
The method was later systematized by European mathematicians in the 
17th and 18th centuries.

marginNotes.text:
Two are better than one, because they 
have a good return for their labor.

marginNotes.author: Descartes
marginNotes.text:
Each problem that I solved became a rule, which 
served afterwards to solve other problems.
